abilities.text: Recall the major structural shift between 2012 and 2022: a drastic decrease in independent private practice and a surge in physicians employed by large health systems.
State the current projected doubling time of medical knowledge as 73 days.
Analyze your clinical workflows to describe how these macroeconomic principles manifest after watching the embedded video.
Identify and summarize the diagram categorizing the domains of care failure from the required reading.
Recall that U.S. healthcare spending is reaching $5.2 trillion in 2024 and describe its structural implications.
Report that roughly 25% of total U.S. healthcare spending goes toward administrative costs and waste.
Recognize that 66% of physicians use some form of AI in practice while formal training to oversee these tools remains severely limited.
Define the digital front door as creating a frictionless journey beyond a basic patient portal.
Report that 67% of physicians want leadership roles but only 18% receive executive or business training.
Summarize that healthcare is shifting toward a system that is data-driven, consumer-responsive, and operationally incredibly complex.

demand.scene_text: You, the attending physician, are managing a complex workflow and you notice a recurring delay in patient transport. Requests sit in the queue and the patient stays in place as the workflow waits for pickup.

research_entries.evidence_excerpt: Health information exchanges (HIEs) are expected to improve poor information coordination in emergency departments (EDs); however, whether and when HIEs are associated with better operational outcomes remains poorly understood. In this work, we study HIE and length of stay (LOS) relationship using a large dataset from the Healthcare Cost and Utilization Project consisting of about 7.4 million treat‐and‐release visits made to 63 EDs in Massachusetts. Overall, we find that HIE adoption is associated with a 10.2% reduction in LOS and the percentage reduction increases to 14.8% when the hospital is part of an integrated health system or to 21.0% when a patient has a previous visit to an HIE‐carrying hospital. We further find that (i) teaching hospitals benefit more from HIE adoption compared with non‐teaching hospitals, (ii) patients with severe or multiple comorbid conditions spend less time in the ED under HIE presence. Together, these results imply that (i) HIE adoption reduces overall ED LOS, (ii) wider HIE adoption would scale up the benefits for individual hospitals, (iii) magnitude of the association between HIE and LOS is higher when financial incentives for HIE adoption are stronger (e.g., integrated health systems), and (iv) the size of the reduction depends on certain contextual moderating factors. Given that HIEs are a key component of healthcare delivery and ongoing reforms, we believe that our findings have important implications and may inform policymakers regarding the nationwide HIE adoption.
research_entries.title: The Impact of Health Information Exchanges on Emergency Department Length of Stay

raw_rows.body: Health information exchanges (HIEs) are expected to improve poor information coordination in emergency departments (EDs); however, whether and when HIEs are associated with better operational outcomes remains poorly understood. In this work, we study HIE and length of stay (LOS) relationship using a large dataset from the Healthcare Cost and Utilization Project consisting of about 7.4 million treat‐and‐release visits made to 63 EDs in Massachusetts. Overall, we find that HIE adoption is associated with a 10.2% reduction in LOS and the percentage reduction increases to 14.8% when the hospital is part of an integrated health system or to 21.0% when a patient has a previous visit to an HIE‐carrying hospital. We further find that (i) teaching hospitals benefit more from HIE adoption compared with non‐teaching hospitals, (ii) patients with severe or multiple comorbid conditions spend less time in the ED under HIE presence. Together, these results imply that (i) HIE adoption reduces overall ED LOS, (ii) wider HIE adoption would scale up the benefits for individual hospitals, (iii) magnitude of the association between HIE and LOS is higher when financial incentives for HIE adoption are stronger (e.g., integrated health systems), and (iv) the size of the reduction depends on certain contextual moderating factors. Given that HIEs are a key component of healthcare delivery and ongoing reforms, we believe that our findings have important implications and may inform policymakers regarding the nationwide HIE adoption.
raw_rows.title: The Impact of Health Information Exchanges on Emergency Department Length of Stay
